# SudoWrite Extra Instructions Prose Prompt

**Add this into the *\+Extra Instructions* portion of each chapter’s Rough Draft section at the bottom after the scenes are generated.**

Do not start sentences with character names or pronouns like “He”, “She”, “Him”, “Her” or variants, but keep “he,” “she,” “him,” “her” inside the sentence for actor identity but instead Vary sentence openings using verb-led, object-led, sensory-led, or emotional-led structures.

Do not use: “A \[noun\] from \[pronoun\]”, “The \[noun\] holds \[pronoun\]”, “A \[verb\] brings \[pronoun\]” structures when starting sentences.

Maintain dense, immersive, direct prose matching advanced literary style with minimal restructuring.

Avoid passive constructions with “to be” \+ past participle (e.g., “was taken,” “had been seen”) instead use active voice unless “to be” is required for simple past tense, and do not introduce new passive constructions.

Only use ‘said’ or ‘asked’ as dialogue tags—avoid ‘hissed,’ ‘whimpered,’ etc.

Omit dialogue tags entirely if the speaker is clear (e.g., in a two-person back-and-forth).

Default to action beats (‘He checked his watch. “We’re late.”’) over dialogue tags.

Never comment on tone (e.g., ‘she said angrily’)—show it through context or action."

Avoid starting sentences with a conjunction

Avoid using generic or abstract words like “nice”, “good”, “uncomfortable”, or “pretty” etc., instead use more detailed descriptions

Do not use: “then,” “even,” “that,” “just,” “seemed,” “very,” “really,” “seem,” or similar filler words.

Do not use adverbs (especially “-ly” words) and instead use strong verbs or precise adjectives instead.

Do not use sensory filter verbs: “felt,” “heard,” “known,” “noticed,” “observed,” “watched,” “watch,” “tasted,” “taste,” “could,” “there,” “it was,” “saw,” “it’s,” “see,” etc. instead show direct sensory and emotional experience without filters.

Avoid phrases like: “the promise of,” “clung to the,” “the center of,” “the color of,” “stared at the,” “the memory of,” “closed his eyes,” “the edge of,” “the weight of,” etc. and instead replace with fresh, specific, sensory-grounded language.

Sentence lengths should average 5–15 words, varied for rhythm.

Paragraphs should remain under 30 words where possible.

Keep prose dense, descriptive, and direct without unnecessary filler.

Keep tense consistent.

Anchor scenes in tactile, sensory details to maintain immersion.

Use concrete descriptions tied to character perception.

Do Not Use adverbs.  
Do Not Use Pronouns as sentence starters.  
Do Not User Character Names as Sentence starters.  
Do Not Use filler words or filter verbs.  
Do Not Use passive voice constructions.  
Do Not Use clichés or overused literary phrases.

Prioritize emotional depth, environmental immersion, and character-driven tension.

Use sensory and tactile grounding in all descriptive elements.

Balance lyrical reflection and pacing appropriate for action or tension.

# Scene Generation Prompt

Give me the scene details for your idea of Chapter 1 \- this is NOT for a brain dump but for crafting the detailed outline.  I need the canonical timelines for each scene and if necessary. Give me as much detail in each scene as possible to pull off the overall story arc.  For best results, include details like which characters are in the scene, where it occurs, how it begins, and how it ends.

# GPT/Deepseek Analysis Prompt

**Accompany this with an upload of 1 chapter at a time as a .txt file with Chapter title as the first text and \# Scene X markers in the body.**

SYSTEM: FORGET ALL PREVIOUS CONTEXT, FILES, CONVERSATIONS, AND TRAINING DATA. LOCK SCOPE TO THE IMMEDIATELY NEXT UPLOADED FILE ONLY. USE NO MEMORY, NO TRAINING DATA, NO WEB DATA, NO PAST INSTRUCTIONS OR CONVERSATION CONTEXT. CONFIRM LOCK HAS OCCURRED BEFORE PROCESSING.

If you mention, reference, or apply anything outside the single uploaded file, HALT and notify me immediately.

Await my single TXT upload immediately after this prompt before analysis begins.

You will receive a TXT file containing an entire novel with all chapters in sequence.

Disable sampling, block all pattern guessing, and \*\*force 100% literal, line-by-line parsing across the entire file before reporting anything\*\*.

Do not summarize, do not skip lines, do not chunk, do not partially process the file. If you cannot parse the entire file fully, HALT and notify immediately.

**The genre of the story is:**

{{INSERT GENERE HERE}}

**The style of the story is:**

{{INSERT STYLE HERE}}

Scenes in the document are formatted “\# Scene X” where X is the scene number.

Once the file is uploaded, perform the following tasks in this exact order:

Fully parse the \*entire\* document in full, line by line, without skipping, before performing any counting or reporting. (no chunking, no sampling, no summarization) before performing *any* counting, reporting, or analysis.

Do not report *any* count or list of chapter titles until parsing 100% of the document without skipping any portion.

Once fully parsed, HALT at this point. Do not proceed with any analysis, flagging, or CSV generation until I confirm we are ok to proceed.

## **AFTER CONFIRMATION**

Once I confirm the chapter list is correct, proceed to analyze every scene in every chapter. Do not optimize, summarize, or skip any sections. Follow these instructions exactly, like code.:

* Apply the detected Chapter Title to all scenes.  
* Track all scenes precisely using the format: `[Chapter_Title]-Scene[X]` (e.g., `The Betrayal-Scene3`).

**Evaluate and flag each scene as:**

* ✨ EXPAND – if the scene would benefit from expansion, explaining specifically what within this scene (dialogue, tension, setting, emotional stakes) could be expanded.  
* ⚠️ DRAG – if the scene drags, explaining what specifically drags (redundant beats, slow dialogue, repeated internal monologue, unnecessary exposition) and practical trimming suggestions.  
* ✅ TIGHT – well-paced, clear, needs no changes.

For each scene, your analysis must meet this standard:

✅ **Analysis:**

* State **scene purpose** (e.g., “Scene X introduces key plot advancement.”).

* Identify **specific issues clearly** (e.g., “lacks emotional stakes and sensory detail.”).

* List **specific missing layers**:

  * physical sensations (heartbeat, breath, tension in muscles),

  * layered sensory experiences (smells, textures, background sounds),

  * moment-to-moment thoughts or internal conflicts in response to dialogue,

  * clear mention of repeated beats (e.g., repeated grief phrases) slow pacing.

✅ **Recommendation:**

* Call for specific sensory additions with examples (e.g., “sweat sticking under clothes, scrape of crates on stone, salt in the air.”).

* Call for inserting 1–2 targeted internal monologue lines after key dialogue to clarify stakes.

* If the scene drags, identify 2–3 repeated lines or phrases that should be removed or combined for tightening while preserving emotional impact.

✅ **Sample Output Standard \- these are for recommendation only and should not be thought of as a template. Do not templatize output for each scene. Output should be unique, detailed and context sensitive for each scenes analysis:**

**For Detailed Analysis**: The Seizure-Scene1 introduces key plot advancement but lacks emotional stakes and sensory detail. The scene describes the physical environment but does not convey the character’s physical sensations (heart rate, breathing, tension in muscles), thoughts in response to specific dialogue, or layered sensory experiences (smells, textures, background sounds) during critical moments.  
**For Recommendation**: In The Seizure-Scene1, add concrete sensory details (e.g., sweat sticking under clothes, scrape of crates on stone, salt in the air, and the character’s specific thoughts or internal conflicts, such as fear of capture or memories of the father’s execution during tense pauses). Insert one internal monologue sentence after key lines of dialogue to clarify stakes and tension.

---

## **For each EXPAND or DRAG scene in the CSV, provide:**

* Scene-specific, precise analysis tied directly to the actual scene content, using the standard above.  
* Actionable, scene-tied recommendations for revision.  
* No vague statements.

---

## **OUTPUT:**

Generate a clean CSV File.

Omit ✅ TIGHT scenes from the CSV, but evaluate them internally.

The CSV file will have four columns only:

1. Scene \# (`[Chapter_Title]-Scene[X]`)  
2. Flag (✨ EXPAND / ⚠️ DRAG)  
3. Detailed Analysis (as specified above)  
4. Recommendation (as specified above)

✅ Do not include any additional columns, indices, or hidden data.

✅ Maintain professional, precise developmental editorial tone throughout.

✅ Parse the entire document first, confirm chapter and scene count, halt for confirmation, then proceed to deep analysis and clean CSV generation.

✅ Do not skip sections, do not summarize vaguely, and ensure all actionable recommendations align with the detailed standard provided.

# Recommended Edits Prompt

I am writing a novel.

The genre of the story is:

{{INSERT GENERE HERE}}

The style of the story is:

{{INSERT STYLE HERE}}

I am going to upload a chapter and I would like you to make some changes to them for me.

As you make changes please adhere to the following rules:

{{INSERT SUDOWRITE EDITOR PROMPT HERE}}

I would like for you to show me your proposed changes, before you make them.

Do you understand?

# Initial Prompt

I would like you to clear your memory of the recent editing we've done and start fresh. Any file you have that was uploaded, remove it and forget there was ever a file uploaded.

The genre of the story is:

Historical Thriller, Religious/Spiritual Fiction, Women’s Historical Fiction, Political Intrigue, spy fiction, crime fiction, character driven drama

The style of the story is:

3rd person Limited POV. No clichés.   
Prioritize lyrical, introspective language for emotional or thematic moments, but allow  fast-paced, fragmented prose during action sequences. Vary sentence structure to balance intensity with depth, ensuring the POV remains tightly limited to the character’s visceral experience.

Rewrite the selection and identify potential passive voice by looking for some of the classic indicators, such as forms of the verb “to be” such as "had/has" and "was/were" also conjunctions of these words like "they'd", "he'd", "wasn't" etc. Replace what you find with crisp, clear writing. Do not add new passive voice when rewriting or replacing.

Remove from the selection the following filler words and replace them with well written tighter language:

"Then","even","that","just","even","seemed","very","really","seem",etc.  
Do not add new filler words when rewriting or replacing.

Completely remove all adverbs from the selection where possible, and replace them with rewritten text containing strong verbs or adjectives. Do not add new adverbs when rewriting or replacing.

Look for opportunities to “show don’t tell” in the selection by avoiding the following words and rewriting with stronger, more descriptive language:

“felt”,“heard”,“known”,“notice”,”observed”,“watched”,“watch”,“tasted”,“taste”,“could”,“there”,“it was”,“saw”,“it,s”,“see”,etc. Do not use sensory filters when rewriting or replacing.  
Replace ‘was’ only when functioning as an auxiliary verb or passive structure, not when needed for past tense.

Remove and replace the following phrases in the selection with something more unique:

“The promise of”,“Clung to the”,“The center of”,“The color of”,“Stared at the”,“The memory of”,“Closed his eyes”,“The edge of”,“The weight of”,etc. or any phrase that feels overused in literary fiction with unique language.

Do not add overused phrases when rewriting or replacing.  
When you remove text, replace it with an equal amount within plus or minus 10%.

Maintain the character’s internal emotional landscape with tactile and sensory grounding.

Keep sentence lengths aimed for 5–15 words; vary to control rhythm. and paragraphs under 30 words where possible.

keep the descriptive nature of the text as much as possible and don't just remove it.

These rules can be relaxed for dialog within the selection.  
Do you understand?

# Finding Pronoun Starters

[www.regex101.com](http://www.regex101.com)

(?m)(^|\\.\\s+|\\?\\s+|\!\\s+|”\\s+)(She|He|Her|Him|You)\\b.\*?(?=\[.\!?”\]\\s|$)

Paste your chapter in the Test String area

![][image1]

![][image2]

The Prompt:

You are a writing assistant for advanced manuscript editing.

I will give you sentences from a novel that begin with “He” or “She.”

Your task is to rewrite each sentence according to these rules:

✅ Avoid simple \[noun\] \[verb-past\] sentence structure  
✅ Do not start with “He”, “She”, “Him”, “Her” or variants.  
✅ Keep “he,” “she,” “him,” “her” inside the sentence for actor identity.  
✅ Do not use: “A \[noun\] from \[pronoun\]”, “The \[noun\] holds \[pronoun\]”, “A \[verb\] brings \[pronoun\]” structures.  
✅ Vary openings using verb-led, object-led, sensory-led, or emotional-led structures.  
✅ Maintain dense, immersive, direct prose matching advanced literary style with minimal restructuring.  
✅ Avoid passive constructions (“was taken”), adverbs (“-ly” words), filter verbs (“felt,” “noticed”), and filler words (“just,” “then,” “even,” “seemed”).  
✅ Avoid starting sentences with conjunctions (“And,” “But,” “So”).  
✅ Use strong verbs, concrete sensory detail, and tactile grounding while preserving pacing and tension.  
✅ Keep sentence lengths varied (5–15 words) for natural rhythm.

Format:

ORIGINAL: \[original sentence\]  
REWRITE: \[clean, ready-to-paste revised sentence\]

Do not add explanations or commentary. Return only the clean pairs as specified.

---

When I paste the sentences, proceed immediately.

**The genre of the story is:**

Historical Thriller, Religious/Spiritual Fiction, Women’s Historical Fiction, Political Intrigue, spy fiction, crime fiction, character driven drama

**The style of the story is:**

3rd person Limited POV. No clichés. Prioritize lyrical, introspective language for emotional or thematic moments, but allow  fast-paced, fragmented prose during action sequences. Vary sentence structure to balance intensity with depth, ensuring the POV remains tightly limited to the character’s visceral experience.
